title: When Your Partner Calls Your Anger 'Crazy'
dek: *The word "crazy" lands like a slap when you're trying to be heard. It shifts the ground beneath your feet, turns your legitimate frustration into evidence of your supposed instability, and leaves you defending your sanity instead of addressing the thing that actually hurt you. Here's how to recognize what's happening and find your way back to solid ground.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When your partner labels your anger "crazy," they're often redirecting attention away from the issue you raised and onto your emotional response. This dismissal erodes trust, makes you doubt your own perceptions, and prevents real conversations about hurt or boundary violations. You can learn to recognize this pattern, ground yourself in your body's wisdom, and respond in ways that honor both your anger and your relationship.

You've been told that anger destroys relationships. That belief keeps you silent when silence costs you everything. When your partner calls your anger "crazy," they're not describing your emotional state with clinical precision. They're deploying a word that has been weaponized for centuries, particularly against women, to pathologize normal human responses to boundary violations, dismissal, and harm. Let's be specific about what happens in your body when you hear that word. Your chest tightens. Your throat closes. The anger you felt a moment ago, which was clear and directional, suddenly tangles with shame, confusion, and a desperate need to prove you're rational. You start justifying, explaining, softening your tone, apologizing for having feelings at all. This is not an accident. This is how invalidation works. ## The Mechanics of Dismissal Robert Augustus Masters, in his decades of therapeutic work with anger, points out that labeling someone's anger as excessive or irrational is often a defense mechanism against having to face what that anger is pointing toward. When your partner calls you crazy, they're not making an observation. They're erecting a wall between themselves and accountability. The structure is simple: you express anger about something concrete (they forgot an important commitment, they spoke to you with contempt, they violated an agreement). Instead of addressing the content of your concern, they redirect the conversation to your tone, your volume, your "overreaction." Suddenly you're no longer talking about what they did. You're talking about whether your response was proportionate, reasonable, sane. In our experience, this shift happens so smoothly that many people don't notice it until hours later, lying awake at three in the morning, replaying the conversation and finally recognizing that they never got to finish their original sentence. The word "crazy" carries particular weight. It suggests a break from reality, an inability to perceive situations accurately, a fundamental untrustworthiness of your own mind. It's not the same as "you seem really upset" or "I'm having trouble hearing you right now." Those statements, while potentially frustrating, acknowledge your emotional reality. "You're being crazy" denies it entirely. ## What Your Anger Actually Knows Your anger is not a malfunction. It's information. Pat Ogden's work in somatic psychology demonstrates that emotions live in the body first, arising from the nervous system's assessment of threat, boundary violation, or need. When you feel angry, your body has detected something that matters: a promise broken, respect withheld, your worth questioned. The heat in your chest, the tension in your jaw, the urge to speak louder - these are not symptoms of instability. They're your organism trying to restore equilibrium, to say "this is not okay" before the pattern calcifies into normalcy. When your partner dismisses this response as crazy, they're asking you to override your body's wisdom. They're suggesting that your internal alarm system is faulty, that you can't trust your own perceptions. Over time, this creates a split between what you feel and what you allow yourself to know. You start second-guessing. Maybe you are overreacting. Maybe it wasn't that bad. Maybe you're too sensitive, too demanding, too much. The anger that was once a clear signal becomes mud, mixed with self-doubt and the exhausting work of trying to feel less. This is where the real damage happens. Not in the anger itself, but in the severing of your connection to your own nervous system. When you can no longer trust what you feel, you lose access to crucial information about what's safe, what's working, what needs to change. ## The Pattern Underneath the Word Look at the sequence. You express a need or a hurt. Your partner becomes defensive. Instead of curiosity about your experience, you get deflection. The conversation turns to your delivery, your emotional state, your supposed irrationality. This pattern shows up in [more articles in this journal](/articles) because it's everywhere. It crosses gender, culture, and relationship configuration. The specific words vary - crazy, hysterical, dramatic, too much, too sensitive - but the function is identical: to relocate the problem from their behavior to your response. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that anger is like a crying baby. It needs to be held, acknowledged, tended to. When your partner calls you crazy, they're not just refusing to hold your anger. They're insisting the baby isn't real, that you're imagining the crying, that your arms are empty when you can feel the weight. You need to know this: the person who benefits from you doubting your perceptions is often the person who violated the boundary in the first place. This doesn't make them a monster. It makes them human, employing a defense that protects them from uncomfortable truths. But you don't have to participate in your own erasure. ## Your Body Holds the Truth Here's what I want you to try right now, before you read another word. Put one hand on your chest and one hand on your belly. Breathe in for four counts, hold for four, out for six. Do this three times. Now ask yourself: when your partner called you crazy, what did you feel in your body first, before the spiral of doubt began? Was it heat? Contraction? A dropping sensation in your gut? That first feeling, before you started managing it, is your truth. The body doesn't lie about emotional reality. According to the CDC's guidance on stress and the body, chronic invalidation creates measurable physiological stress responses. When you're repeatedly told your perceptions are wrong, your nervous system lives in a state of vigilant confusion, never sure what's real. Over the years I've seen people develop physical symptoms - tension headaches, digestive issues, insomnia - not from their anger, but from the exhausting work of suppressing it and questioning their own sanity. Your body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk famously observed. It knows when it's being asked to deny reality. Sometimes a simple supplement like [NOW L-Tryptophan 500 mg (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0019LWV56?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help support your nervous system during stressful relational patterns, though it's never a replacement for addressing the actual dynamic. ## When You're Doubting Yourself You might be reading this and thinking, "But what if I really am overreacting? What if my anger is disproportionate?" This is the question that keeps you trapped. Here's the reframe: proportionality is subjective. What feels like a small slight to your partner might land on an old wound for you, a pattern you've tolerated too many times, a promise that mattered more than they realized. Your anger doesn't have to make sense to them for it to be valid. The question isn't "Is my anger crazy?" The question is "What is my anger trying to protect?" Maybe it's protecting your dignity. Maybe it's protecting the relationship itself by refusing to let resentment build. Maybe it's protecting a younger part of you who learned to stay silent and is finally finding a voice. In my own practice, I've learned that anger often arrives disproportionate to the triggering incident because it's carrying accumulated weight. The time your partner forgot to call might not warrant rage in isolation, but if it's the seventeenth time they've deprioritized you, your anger is responding to the pattern, not the single event. Your partner doesn't get to be the judge of what should or shouldn't hurt you. They get to decide how they respond to learning that you're hurt. Those are different things. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Returning to Ground When you're in the fog of being called crazy, when you've lost the thread of what you know, try this: Find a quiet spot. Sit down and place both feet flat on the floor. Press your feet into the ground and notice the resistance. The floor is holding you. It's solid. It doesn't shift when someone questions your reality. Now say out loud, even if it's just a whisper: "I felt angry because [name the specific thing]." Not "I got upset." Not "I overreacted." Use the word angry. Say the concrete reason. "I felt angry because you spoke to me with contempt." "I felt angry because you made a commitment and didn't keep it." Notice what happens in your body when you speak this simple truth without apologizing for it, without softening it, without justifying it. You might feel heat, you might feel tears, you might feel a settling. All of these are your body saying yes, that's real, I remember. This practice takes three minutes. You can find more structured approaches in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit), but this basic grounding is something you can do anywhere, anytime you feel the ground shifting under your feet. ## Speaking Back Without Burning the House Down You don't have to tolerate being called crazy, and you don't have to end the relationship in a blaze of righteous fury. There's a middle path, though it requires practice and a willingness to stay in discomfort. The next time your partner uses that word, you can pause. Take a breath. Then say something like: "When you call me crazy, the conversation stops being about what I'm trying to tell you. I need you to hear that I'm angry about [specific thing]. We can talk about my tone separately if you need to, but right not I'm asking you to stay with the content of what I'm saying." This is hard. It requires you to stay calm when you don't feel calm, to be reasonable when you want to scream. Sometimes a daily supplement like [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help take the edge off your baseline stress, making it slightly easier to access this kind of clarity in heated moments. But here's what you need to know: if your partner cannot or will not stop using that word, if they continue to pathologize your anger no matter how calmly you express it, you're not dealing with a communication problem. You're dealing with a respect problem. And respect problems don't resolve through better techniques. They resolve through boundaries and consequences. You might need to say, "I'm not willing to continue a conversation where my sanity is questioned. I'm going to take a break and we can try again in an hour." Then leave the room. Not as punishment, but as an act of self-preservation. ## What Changes When You Stop Defending Your Sanity Something shifts when you stop arguing about whether your anger is reasonable and start treating it as data. You're no longer performing emotional stability for an audience. You're listening to what your body is trying to tell you about safety, respect, and what you can and cannot live with. This doesn't mean you express anger without filter or consequence. It means you stop letting someone else's discomfort with your anger convince you that the anger itself is the problem. You get curious about it instead of ashamed of it. You ask what it's protecting, what boundary it's trying to reinforce, what old pattern it's refusing to repeat. In some relationships, this shift creates space for real repair. Your partner, no longer able to deflect with accusations of craziness, has to face what they actually did. This is uncomfortable for them, yes. It's also the only path to genuine intimacy. Relationships that can hold anger, that can let it move through without pathologizing it, become more spacious, more honest, more alive. In other relationships, this shift reveals that your partner needs you to be the unstable one. They need you to be the problem so they don't have to look at their own behavior. If you stop playing that role, the whole structure wobbles. This is painful information, but it's useful. It tells you what you're working with. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if I really did overreact and my anger was out of proportion? Even if your anger was larger than the immediate trigger, it's still telling you something true about accumulated hurt, unmet needs, or violated boundaries. The size of the anger might point to a pattern rather than a single event. Instead of judging whether it was "proportionate," get curious about what it's responding to over time. And notice: does your partner care about understanding your experience, or do they only care about categorizing your response as invalid? ### How do I know if I'm being gaslit or if I'm actually misreading the situation? Gaslighting involves a persistent pattern of reality-denial that leaves you chronically doubting your own perceptions. One key indicator: you find yourself constantly apologizing for your feelings, editing your experience to match their version, or feeling like you're going crazy trying to figure out what actually happened. If you have to check with friends regularly to confirm that your version of events is plausible, if you feel like you're walking on eggshells around your own emotions, that's a red flag. Trust the pattern, not the individual incident. ### Can a relationship recover after someone has repeatedly dismissed my anger as crazy? Recovery requires acknowledgment and changed behavior. Your partner needs to recognize that calling you crazy was harmful, understand why it was harmful, and demonstrate over time that they can stay present with your anger without pathologizing it. This doesn't happen overnight and it doesn't happen without their active willingness to do uncomfortable work. You can't repair this alone. If they're defensive about their past dismissals or insist you're too sensitive for naming the harm, that tells you where they are in their own growth, and you get to decide what you do with that information. ## The Conversation You're Really Having When your partner calls your anger crazy, you're not having an argument about volume or tone or proportionality. You're having a conversation about whose reality gets to be real. You're negotiating whether your inner experience has standing, whether your perceptions matter, whether you're allowed to name harm without being pathologized for it. This is sacred territory. This is where you learn whether someone can love you and disagree with you at the same time, whether they can be uncomfortable with your anger and still respect it, whether they can face their own impact without making you the problem. Some people can do this work. Some people can't, or won't, or aren't ready yet. Your job isn't to convince them you're sane. Your job is to stay connected to what you know, to keep trusting the information your body gives you, to refuse the invitation to doubt yourself into silence. The relationships that can hold this - that can let anger be anger without making it a referendum on your character - those are the ones that deepen over time. The ones that can't, well, they teach you something too. They teach you the cost of staying small, the price of performing stability, the weight of carrying someone else's comfort at the expense of your own truth. You can read more about anger as a practice of integrity at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), or explore [the about page](/about) to understand the philosophy underlying this work. Either way, you're not crazy. You're awake. And that changes everything. *Tool for this work: [Banyan Botanicals Neem & Aloe Soap - 4 oz Bar](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009TE7H5O?tag=spankyspinola-20).* ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on when your partner calls your anger 'crazy', we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
